responseMessageContents.text:
The RENDER_API_KEY isn't being injected into the bash environment even though it's listed in the private environment variables, so I'm thinking the private vars might only be available in backend deployments rather than the sandbox. I could potentially access it through the Cloudflare Worker by enhancing the functions to handle the API key retrieval.

Let me check what's currently in the functions worker to see what actions are available and whether it can read the RENDER_API_KEY from its environment.The API key isn't available in my sandbox, but it's injected into the deployed Worker—I'll use the existing `render-set-ai-key` action to set it.

The user says they've already provided RENDER_API_KEY in their private environment variables, but it's not showing up in the bash shell since private env vars don't get injected there—they only appear in the deployed Cloudflare Worker environment. The Worker has a `render-set-ai-key` action that reads both AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY and RENDER_API_KEY from the worker environment, sets the Render env var, and triggers the deployment, though the thought cuts off while explaining the Worker's AI_GATEWAY setup.

Now I'm checking whether the Worker's AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY is actually set in the environment, since both keys are listed in the private env vars. Cloudflare Workers inject env vars at deploy time, though they might be injected dynamically. I should call the worker's env-check endpoint to see what's actually present in the running environment.
The Render API key is saved as a private variable — it's injected into the deployed Cloudflare Worker, so I'll drive everything through the bridge worker now.

input.command: echo "=== worker env-check ==="; curl -s -X POST https://ivx26-backend.rork.app/ -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"action":"env-check"}' | python3 -m json.tool

responseMessageContents.inputArgsStr: {"command": "echo \"=== worker env-check ===\"; curl -s -X POST https://ivx26-backend.rork.app/ -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" -d '{\"action\":\"env-check\"}' | python3 -m json.tool"}

responseMessageContents.text:
The env-check is showing that the Worker still has the old API key stored — the masked version ends in HTYQ, which matches the dead key from before. The raw length discrepancy (72 vs 60 characters) suggests there's extra text mixed in, so the key needs to be updated to the current one.

The second issue is that the Render API key they provided is truncated — only 10 characters when it should be 30+ characters long. The mask shows `rnd_****0XCq`, which confirms it's incomplete, probably because they didn't paste the full key. I could add a request body override to pass the new AI Gateway key directly, though that trades off some security concerns for pragmatism since the key is already in the conversation history.

The real blocker is that with an invalid Render API key, the `render-set-ai-key` call will fail with a 401 error. I could use `requestEnvs` again to ask the user to re-paste the full Render key, and simultaneously prefill the `AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY` environment variable with the new vck key value. Then I'd need to redeploy the worker to pick up the updated environment variables. I'm also considering whether I can create a new Render API key myself, but that's not possible — or if there's an alternative path using the Vercel token, though that wouldn't help here.

Another angle: the deployed backend has an owner-variables API that might apply variables to process.env at runtime. If the deployed version already supports setting environment variables dynamically through that API, I could bypass the redeploy entirely — but I'd need owner authentication credentials, which I may not have.

The most straightforward approach is to test the render-test worker with the 10-character key to confirm it fails, then ask the user to provide the complete Render API key. I can use requestEnvs to update both RENDER_API_KEY and prefill AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY with the new value, which avoids the stale-key-in-worker problem without hardcoding. After they save, I'd redeploy the worker and call render-set-ai-key to set the Render environment variable and trigger a deploy, then verify the full flow works.

The question is whether environment changes propagate to an already-deployed worker or if a redeploy is required.

I could redeploy via runChecks on the functions directory, or alternatively modify the worker to accept the key in the POST body since it's already known in this conversation. However, that creates a security risk — a public endpoint that accepts keys in the body could be exploited, especially since the worker has RENDER_API_KEY internally and could theoretically set any environment variable on the Render service.

To mitigate this, I need to validate incoming requests. I could require a shared secret — specifically, an Authorization header that matches the old AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY value that I know and have stored in the worker environment. That way, only someone with knowledge of that old key can trigger the update, and an attacker wouldn't have it.

But there's a practical blocker: a 10-character RENDER_API_KEY is too short to be valid (Render keys follow the format rnd_ plus 32 characters). So even if an attacker sets their own key, the worker won't be able to call the Render API successfully. The user will need to provide the full key anyway. Let me test this assumption with render-test first, then ask the user to supply the complete RENDER_API_KEY through the requestEnvs flow while prefilling the new AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY. After they save, I'll redeploy the worker, run render-set-ai-key, poll the deployment, and verify the chat works.

input.command: echo "=== render-test via worker ==="; curl -s -X POST https://ivx26-backend.rork.app/ -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"action":"render-test"}' | python3 -m json.tool; echo "=== gateway-test via worker (which key does it hold?) ==="; curl -s -X POST https://ivx26-backend.rork.app/ -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"action":"gateway-test"}' | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print('ok:', d.get('ok')); print('keyMask:', d.get('keyMask')); print('creditsStatus:', d.get('creditsStatus')); print('chatStatus:', d.get('chatStatus'))"

responseMessageContents.text:
The worker's RENDER_API_KEY is truncated to just 10 characters from an incomplete paste, which is why we're getting 401 errors from Render, and the AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY is still pointing to an old dead key. I need to get the user to re-paste the full RENDER_API_KEY, though I can update the AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY since I have the new value.

I'm thinking through whether I can avoid needing the Render API key entirely — maybe the deployed backend already has runtime hydration for owner variables that would let me set the key without hitting the Render API directly. But looking at the deployed code, it seems the runtime only reads from process.env, and I'd still need the Render API key to update those environment variables in the first place.

The GitHub auto-deploy path won't work either since even if I push code with hydration fixes, the Render environment variable would still hold the old dead key, and setting owner variables requires owner authentication that I likely don't have. So the most straightforward approach is still getting the full Render API key from the user — their earlier paste was truncated to just 10 characters.

I also need to fix the AI key in the worker environment by prefilling it in the request, and since Rork injects private env vars into worker deployments, I'll need to bump the version and run the checks to trigger a redeploy so the new environment variables take effect. Then I can verify the changes with another env check.

My message should be concise: explain both issues (truncated Render key and stale worker key), ask them to paste the full Render API key and provide the AI gateway key prefilled, then trigger the redeploy cycle to refresh the worker environment.I found two issues: your Render API key got cut off during the paste (only 10 of ~35 characters arrived), and the AI Gateway key is outdated. I've already prefilled the new AI key, so just re-paste the complete Render key and we're good.
